content.text:
All 20 tests in the new suite pass. `test/merge.test.js` covers every documented behavior with tests that would each catch a concrete bug:

- **Empty/trivial:** `[] -> []`, single interval, `start === end` (valid, zero-width).
- **Overlap:** partial overlap, full containment (guards the `Math.max` — using `end` alone would shrink `[1,10]` to `[1,3]`), transitive chaining against the running merged end.
- **Touching-vs-disjoint boundary** (the `start <= last.end` threshold): at the threshold (`[1,2],[2,3]` merge), just above (`[1,2],[3,4]` unit gap stays separate), and clearly disjoint.
- **Sorting:** unsorted input ordered; intervals that only overlap after sorting; a scrambled mix — each hand-computed.
- **Duplicates:** collapse to one.
- **Negatives & floats:** negative-spanning merge; float pair just short of touching stays separate.
- **Errors:** `start > end` throws `RangeError`, `start === end` does not, and an invalid pair that isn't first still throws (catches validating only the first element).
- **Immutability:** input array/pairs unchanged, and output pairs aren't references back into the input.

Every value is computed by hand from the spec rather than copied from the implementation. I left the starter `example.test.js` untouched since it passes and I was asked not to touch `src/merge.js`/`package.json`; the real coverage lives in `merge.test.js`.

- **Empty/trivial:** `[] -> []`, single interval, `start === end` (valid, zero-width).
- **Overlap:** partial overlap, full containment (guards the `Math.max` — using `end` alone would shrink `[1,10]` to `[1,3]`), transitive chaining against the running merged end.
- **Touching-vs-disjoint boundary** (the `start <= last.end` threshold): at the threshold (`[1,2],[2,3]` merge), just above (`[1,2],[3,4]` unit gap stays separate), and clearly disjoint.
- **Sorting:** unsorted input ordered; intervals that only overlap after sorting; a scrambled mix — each hand-computed.
- **Duplicates:** collapse to one.
- **Negatives & floats:** negative-spanning merge; float pair just short of touching stays separate.
- **Errors:** `start > end` throws `RangeError`, `start === end` does not, and an invalid pair that isn't first still throws (catches validating only the first element).
- **Immutability:** input array/pairs unchanged, and output pairs aren't references back into the input.

Every value is computed by hand from the spec rather than copied from the implementation. I left the starter `example.test.js` untouched since it passes and I was asked not to touch `src/merge.js`/`package.json`; the real coverage lives in `merge.test.js`.
